petitioner_background: The petitioner is a karate athlete seeking classification as an alien of extraordinary ability under EB-1A (INA § 203(b)(1)(A)). He claims to have competed nationally and internationally since 2002, when he was 11 years old, and to have won several gold, silver, and bronze medals during that time. The record suggests ties to Russia (Russian-language publications and a Russian karate coach are referenced). The Nebraska Service Center denied the petition, and the matter came before the AAO on appeal.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner claimed membership in a karate team/club (name redacted). He submitted a letter on the club's letterhead stating: 'Admission to [the club] is granted exclusive[ly] to those who have demonstrated a consistent track record of outstanding achievements in this sport. [The Petitioner] was admitted to the club on the basis of his outstanding achievements as judged by nationally or internationally recognized experts in this sport.' The letter referred to the club's bylaws but the bylaws themselves were not submitted. The letterhead included the logo of the International Federation of Karate, and the letter stated that the club belongs to that Federation. Another letter from the same writer identified him as 'the Executive Director of the [redacted] branch of [redacted].'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the evidence insufficient because merely repeating the language of the statute or regulations does not satisfy a petitioner's burden of proof (citing Fedin Bros. Co., Ltd. v. Sava). The letter referenced bylaws but the petitioner did not submit that document or provide information (such as a web address) to permit verification of the claimed membership requirements. The letter provided the name of the signer but not his title at the club, and the record did not establish whether the club employed the writer or granted him authority to make attestations on the club's behalf. Although the letterhead referenced the International Federation of Karate, the record did not contain documentation directly from the Federation to support the claimed affiliation. On appeal, the petitioner repeated the claim but identified no record evidence to support it.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted copies of several articles. These included a newspaper that publishes only 1,000 copies per issue, a Russian-language magazine published in the United States, a publication whose title translates to 'Health' in English (containing headlines about diet, probiotics, and other health-related topics), and a publication the petitioner previously described as a 'weekly socio-political newspaper.' He also submitted information about the Russian news agency Ria Novosti, with an associated article that appeared on rus4all.ru, along with a list of the 'Top Sites in Russia' (which did not include rus4all).
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner did not show the articles appeared in professional or major trade publications or other major media. He did not provide comparative evidence regarding the significance of circulation figures — one newspaper only publishes 1,000 copies per issue, and the Russian-language magazine published in the U.S. reaches only a small portion of the population that reads Russian. The petitioner did not establish rus4all.ru's connection with Ria Novosti or show that the website constitutes major media, and the submitted 'Top Sites in Russia' list did not include rus4all. On appeal, the petitioner claimed the articles appeared in 'major sport-related newspapers,' but had not previously characterized them that way — one was about health topics and another was described as a 'weekly socio-political newspaper.' The AAO found these inconsistent characterizations underscored the lack of persuasive supporting evidence.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted letters from individuals attesting to his skill and accomplishment as an athlete. The chief instructor of a Karate Academy stated: 'In my opinion [the Petitioner] is one of the best Karate master[s] in the World and his technique is truly unique. He has an outstanding background at the highest level of sport and has been a winner [of] European and Asian championship[s], and many times champion of his own country.' A karate coach in Russia, who did not claim any personal knowledge of the petitioner's achievements but 'reviewed [the Petitioner's] affidavit along with the supporting documents,' stated that the petitioner 'has clearly made a substantial contribution into this field by virtue of his unique fighting style and substantial athletic achievements.' The petitioner also argued that winning major national and international competitions itself constituted an original contribution.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO held that petitioners must establish not only that contributions were original but also of major significance in the field — for example, by showing the contributions have been widely implemented, have remarkably impacted or influenced the field, or have otherwise risen to major significance (citing Silverman v. Eastrich). The letters did not identify any specific athletic contributions or explain how they were of major significance. Winning a competition, at whatever level, is not inherently an original contribution of major significance — calling a technique 'truly unique' does not explain what the contributions are or why they are significant. The Russian coach's letter lacked personal knowledge and provided no specific details. The AAO rejected the petitioner's due-process argument regarding expert testimony, noting the letters did not identify any specific contribution or establish major significance. Vague references to 'unique fighting style' were insufficient. Further, prizes and awards fall under a separate criterion at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(i); the regulations contemplate a difference between winning a prize and making an original contribution, and the resulting prize is not self-evident proof of such a contribution.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner initially claimed to meet this criterion for participation as a judge of the work of others, but the specific evidence is not detailed in the decision because he did not contest the Director's conclusion on appeal.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The petitioner did not contest the Director's conclusions regarding criterion (iv) on appeal. Therefore, the AAO considered the issue to be abandoned, citing Matter of R-A-M-, 25 I&N Dec. 657, 658 n.2 (BIA 2012), and related cases.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal because the petitioner did not submit the required initial evidence of either a one-time achievement or documents meeting at least three of the ten regulatory criteria at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3). Because the petitioner did not meet the initial evidentiary threshold, the AAO did not need to conduct the final merits determination referenced in Kazarian. Nevertheless, the AAO reviewed the record in the aggregate and concluded it did not support a finding that the petitioner had established the acclaim and recognition required for the classification sought. The AAO reserved the remaining claimed criteria (awards and leading/critical role) and the separate issues of continued work in the field and substantial prospective benefit to the United States, because resolving them could not change the outcome.
legal_citations: Fedin Bros. Co., Ltd. v. Sava, 724 F. Supp. 1103 (E.D.N.Y. 1989), aff'd, 905 F.2d 41 (2d Cir. 1990)
Silverman v. Eastrich Multiple Investor Fund, L.P., 51 F.3d 28 (3rd Cir. 1995)
Sepulveda v. U.S. Att'y Gen., 401 F.3d 1226 (11th Cir. 2005)
United States v. Cunningham, 161 F.3d 1343 (11th Cir. 1998)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Procedural issues: (1) In response to the Director's RFE identifying deficiencies, the petitioner did not address the deficiencies and instead asked the Director to consider the petition under a different immigrant classification; the Director properly denied this request because there is no provision to allow a change of classification after filing, and one filing fee does not entitle a petitioner to multiple adjudications. (2) The petitioner abandoned the judging criterion by failing to contest the Director's conclusion on appeal. (3) The AAO noted inconsistent characterizations of the published materials across filings (e.g., previously described as a 'weekly socio-political newspaper' but recharacterized on appeal as a 'sport-related newspaper'), which undermined credibility of the supporting evidence.
